elements.sent-2411-001: Twisted torus knots are a generalization of torus knots, obtained by introducing additional full twists to adjacent strands of the torus knots.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this article, we present an explicit formula for the Alexander polynomial of twisted torus knots.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach utilizes a presentation of the knot group of twisted torus knots combined with Fox’s free differential calculus.
elements.sent-2411-004: As applications, we provide a lower bound for the genus of certain families of twisted torus knots and identify families of twisted torus knots that are not $L$-space knots.
elements.sent-2411-005: Given a quadruple of integers $(p,q;r,s)$ such that $\gcd(p,q)=1$, $2\leq r<p$ and $s\in\mathbb{Z}$, the twisted torus knot $T(p,q;r,s)$ is a generalization of torus knots, achieved by applying $s$ full twists to $r$ adjacent strands of the standard diagram of the torus knot $T(p,q)$.
elements.sent-2411-006: This knot can also be described as the closure of the braid corresponding to the braid word \begin{equation}(\sigma_1\sigma_2\dots\sigma_{p-1})^q(\sigma_1\dots\sigma_{r-1})^{rs}\end{equation} with $p$ strands.
elements.sent-2411-007: Figure \ref{fig:TTK illustrates an example of the twisted torus knot $T(7,4;3,2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Note that $T(p,q;r,s)$ is isotopic to $T(q,p;r,s)$, and $T(p,-q;r,s)$ is isotopic to the mirror image of $T(p,q;r,-s)$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Considering our main interests in this paper, such as the Alexander polynomial and the genus of a knot, are invariant under the mirror imaging of a knot, we will assume that $p>q>0$ with $\gcd(p,q)=1$, $1<r<p$, and $s\in\mathbb{Z}$ for twisted torus knot $T(p,q;r,s)$ throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-010: Twisted torus knots were first introduced by Dean in his study of Seifert fibered Dehn surgery \cite{Dean-1996, Dean-2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Since then, twisted torus knots have provided interesting examples of small hyperbolic knots \cite{CDW-1999, CKP-2004 , knots that admit unique Heegaard splitting of their complements \cite{Moriah-Sedgwick-2009 , and hyperbolic knots inducing certain types of hyperbolic structures \cite{Himeo-Teragaito-2023 , among other properties.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: They have also been studied in the context of the $L$-space conjecture as examples of $L$-spaces knots \cite{Vafaee-2015, CGHV-2016, Tran-2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The geometric types of twisted torus knots have been intensively studied by Lee, and also by Lee and Paiva.
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, the classification of twisted torus knots that are the unknot was completely achieved in \cite{Lee-2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: It is also known that some classes of twisted torus knots are, in fact, torus knots \cite{Lee-2019, Lee-2021, Lee-Paiva-2022 , although the complete classification of twisted torus knots that are torus knots has not yet been fully resolved.}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, not much is known about the polynomial knot invariants for twisted torus knots.
elements.sent-2411-017: Since some twisted torus knots are known to be the unknot, or more generally, torus knots as mentioned above, the Alexander polynomials of twisted torus knots in those families are implicitly known.
elements.sent-2411-018: In \cite{Morton-2006 , Morton provided an explicit formula for the Alexander polynomial of twisted torus knots of the type $T(p,q;2,s)$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: His main technique involved the Burau representation alongside an inductive argument based on the skein relation of the Alexander polynomial.

elements.sent-2411-029: Let $p$ and $q$ be relatively prime integers with $p>q>0$, and let $1< r<p$ and $s\in\mathbb{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The Alexander polynomial of the twisted torus knot $T(p,q;r,s)$ is given, up to multiplication by $\pm t^{\pm n}$, as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta_{T(p,q;r,s)}(t)=\frac{1-t}{(1-t^p)(1-t^q)(1-t^{r})}\left(\tilde{X}(t)Y(t)-X(t)\tilde{Y}(t)\right),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}X(t) =1-(1-t^{rs})\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}t^{\overline{k_i}p+(i-1)rs}-t^{pq+(r-1)rs}, \tilde{X}(t) =1-(1-t^{rs})\sum_{i=1}^{m}t^{\overline{k_i}p+(i-1)rs}-t^{\overline{k'}p+mrs}, Y(t) =1-(1-t^{rs})\sum_{i=1}^{r-1}t^{Q_iq+(i-1)rs}-t^{pq+(r-1)rs}, and \tilde{Y}(t) =1-(1-t^{rs})\sum_{i=1}^{m}t^{Q_iq+(i-1)rs}-t^{Q'q+mrs}.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-002: Fundamental Group of Twisted Torus Knots
elements.sent-2411-072: In this section, we utilize the standard genus two Heegaard decomposition of $S^3$ along with Seifert-Van Kampen's theorem to provide a presentation of the knot group of the twisted torus knot $T(p,q;r,s)$.
elements.sent-2411-073: To accurately describe this presentation, it is necessary to define some notations relating to the residue arithmetic of $p$, $q$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-074: For the remainder of our discussion, we will fix the tuple $(p,q;r,s)$ under the conditions that $p>q>0$ with $\gcd(p,q)=1$, $1< r<p$, and $s\in\mathbb{Z}$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let $[x]$ denote the residue of $x$ modulo $p$, i.e. the unique integer $[x]$ such that $0 \leq [x] < p$ and $[x]\equiv x$ modulo $p$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Define $Q$ as the set of residues of the multiples $nq^{-1}$, $n=0,\dots,r-1$ of $q^{-1}$ modulo $p$, where $q^{-1}$ denotes the multiplicative inverse of $q$ modulo $p$.
elements.sent-2411-077: The elements of $Q$ are ordered in ascending sequences as $Q_0,\dots,Q_{r-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-078: This set can be expressed as: \begin{equation}Q:=\{[nq^{-1}]|n=0,\dots,r-1\}=\{Q_0<Q_1<\dots<Q_{r-1}\}.\end{equation}. Define \begin{equation}d_i:=Q_i-Q_{i-1}  for  i=1,\dots,r-1,  and\end{equation} \begin{equation}d_r:=p-Q_{r-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-079: Let $R$ be the set of residues of multiples $-nq^{-1}$ for $n=1,\dots,q$ of $-q^{-1}$ modulo $p$.
elements.sent-2411-080: This can be expressed as: \begin{equation}R:=\{[-nq^{-1}]|n=1,\dots,q\}\end{equation} Define $k_i$ as the cardinality of the set of elements $l$ in $R$ such that $Q_{i-1}\leq l<Q_i$ for $i=1,\dots,r-1$, and $k_r$ as the cardinality of the set of elements $l$ in $R$ satisfying $Q_{r-1}\leq l$. \begin{equation}k_i:=\left|\{l\in R|Q_{i-1}\leq l<Q_i\}\right| for  i=1,\dots,r-1, and \end{equation} \begin{equation}k_r:= \left|\{l\in R|Q_{r-1}\leq l\}\right|.\end{equation}. Define $Q':=[rq^{-1 ]$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Note that $Q'$ is not contained in $Q$ since we assume $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime.
elements.sent-2411-082: Consequently, we have the ordering: \begin{equation}Q_0<\dots<Q_m<Q'<Q_{m+1}<\dots<Q_{r-1}\end{equation} for some $0\leq m\leq r-1$.
